title: Why Gradients Explode in Recurrence
concept: If recurrent weights are slightly greater than 1, repeated multiplication causes gradients to grow exponentially, destabilizing training.

exercises.prompt: If you repeatedly multiply a number by itself, what must that number be for the result to grow larger and larger?
exercises.explain: If you multiply any number by 1, it stays the same. If you multiply by a number less than 1, it shrinks. Only multiplying by a number greater than 1 makes it grow.

cards.title: The Feedback Screech
cards.body: Have you ever turned up a microphone too close to a speaker, and heard that sudden, earsplitting screech? That is feedback loop overload! In Recurrent Neural Networks, a similar explosion happens to our math updates, causing the AI to completely lose its mind during training.

cards.title: Exploding Gradients
cards.body: Exploding Gradients occur in recurrent neural networks when the weights used to connect steps over time are larger than 1. During training, we multiply numbers by these weights over and over. Just like multiplying 1.1 by itself many times, the final number grows infinitely large, causing the network's updates to wildy overshoot.

cards.title: The Magical Echo
cards.body: Imagine walking down a hallway with a magical echo. Every time the sound of your footsteps bounces off the walls, it gets slightly louder instead of softer. Within a few steps, a tiny tap of your foot becomes a deafening roar that shakes the whole building. That is what happens to gradients when they keep multiplying by a number greater than one!

cards.title: Math in Action
cards.body:
If your recurrent weight is 1.5, and you process a short sequence of just 10 words, the gradient gets multiplied by 1.5 ten times in a row. 

1.5 multiplied by itself 10 times is about 57! 

If the sequence was 50 words long, that multiplier becomes over 600,000,000! Your network's computer brain gets overwhelmed by these massive numbers.

cards.title: Myth: Bigger Updates are Better
cards.body: Many beginners think that having larger numbers and bigger updates will help the AI learn much faster. In reality, massive updates cause the model to make wild, unstable guesses. It is like steering a car by violently spinning the wheel all the way left and right—you will just crash!

cards.title: The Weight Threshold
cards.body: Look at how the gradient behaves over time steps depending on the size of the recurrent weight.

exercises.prompt: Why are Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) especially prone to exploding gradients compared to simple feedforward networks?

options.text: Because RNNs reuse the same weights at every time step, leading to repeated multiplication.
Because RNNs always use larger datasets than other types of neural networks.
Because RNNs use division instead of multiplication to update their weights.
Because RNNs only have one single layer of neurons.

exercises.explain: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) share the same weights across all time steps. When we backpropagate through time, we multiply by these same weights repeatedly, which acts like an exponent.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A recurrent weight only slightly greater than 1 (like 1.1) can still cause gradients to explode over long sequences.
exercises.explain: Even a tiny value like 1.1 will blow up if you multiply it by itself dozens or hundreds of times (e.g., 1.1 multiplied 50 times is over 117).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe how recurrent gradients grow when weights are greater than 1.
exercises.sentence: Repeatedly multiplying by weights greater than 1 causes gradients to grow exponentially.
exercises.explain: Because recurrent networks reuse the same weights at every step, any weight value greater than 1 acts like a multiplier that makes the gradients grow exponentially over time.

exercises.prompt: Which AI assistant correctly explains why gradients explode in recurrent networks?

options.text: AI Writer A: "If the recurrent weights are larger than 1, gradients grow exponentially, causing unstable training."
AI Writer B: "If recurrent weights are larger than 1, gradients shrink to zero, causing the network to stop learning."
AI Writer C: "Gradients explode because the network runs out of memory to store negative numbers."

exercises.explain: Only AI Writer A correctly describes the exploding gradient problem where weights greater than 1 lead to runaway exponential growth. B describes the vanishing gradient problem, and C is factually incorrect.

exercises.prompt: What actually happens to the training of a recurrent neural network when gradients explode?

options.text: They blow up exponentially, making weights change by massive amounts and ruining training.
They shrink down to zero, causing the network to stop learning completely.
They stay perfectly stable because the network automatically balances them.
They turn into negative numbers, forcing the network to unlearn everything.

exercises.explain: When recurrent weights are even slightly greater than 1, multiplying them repeatedly over many time steps causes the gradients to grow exponentially. This turns the updates into massive numbers (often showing up as NaN or 'Not a Number' in code), which completely ruins the training process.
